meta.name: SVT Not Responding to Vagal Maneuvers

history.hpi: 32-year-old male software developer who suddenly developed a 'fluttering' sensation in his chest while sitting at his desk approximately 35 minutes ago. Symptoms started abruptly with no precipitating activity, no chest trauma, no recent illness. Patient reports the rate 'just took off' out of nowhere. Also describes mild central chest tightness, lightheadedness, and shortness of breath. No syncope. No prior cardiac history. Drank one large coffee earlier this morning. No recreational drug use reported, no energy drinks, no stimulant supplements. Has had 'a couple of similar shorter episodes' in the past year that self-resolved within minutes, never sought care. Becomes progressively more anxious as scenario unfolds, fearing 'something is really wrong with my heart.'
history.lastOralIntake: Coffee and a granola bar approximately 2 hours ago
history.events: Sitting at desk at work, sudden onset of palpitations. Coworker noticed patient looked pale and called 911. Patient was unable to terminate the episode by 'bearing down' on his own.

scene.dispatch: 32-year-old male, conscious and alert, complaining of palpitations and heart racing. Office building, 4th floor.
scene.location: Open-plan tech office on the 4th floor of a downtown commercial building. Patient seated at his workstation, leaning forward with one hand on his chest. Coworker on scene who placed the 911 call.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Office is calm, coworkers cleared a path. Elevator access available.
scene.bystanders: Coworker (Megan) called 911 and reports patient said 'my heart is going crazy' and looked pale. No witnessed loss of consciousness.
visualCues: Patient seated upright, mildly diaphoretic forehead
Patient appears anxious but is oriented and conversational
No vomit, no spilled medications, no obvious trauma

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: Crew arrives to find a 32-year-old male seated at his desk, alert and conversational, mildly diaphoretic, holding one hand to his chest. He reports a sudden 'racing heart' that started about 35 minutes ago while working. He is mildly short of breath but talking in full sentences. Skin warm and pink. Radial pulse is rapid and regular, too fast to count by palpation. Initial monitor placement reveals a narrow-complex tachycardia at ~190 with no discernible P waves — supraventricular tachycardia. He is hemodynamically stable: BP 112/72, A&Ox3, SpO2 99% on room air.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent, self-maintained, speaking in full sentences
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Mildly tachypneic, no distress, breath sounds clear and equal bilaterally
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Radial pulse rapid and regular — too fast to count; cap refill <2s; skin warm, pink, mildly diaphoretic
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities normally, no focal deficits
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'My heart just took off out of nowhere — I was just sitting here working. It feels like it's going to come out of my chest.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey, ABCs; confirm patient stable, alert, perfusing
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Presentation: Patients will manifest elevated heart rate for age and may or may not also present with associated signs or symptoms such as palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, syncope/near-syncope, hemodynamic compromise, altered mental status, or other signs of end organ malperfusion.
expectedActions.rationale: Determines stable vs unstable — drives the entire pathway. Stable + alert + perfusing = medical management; unstable = cardioversion.

expectedActions.shortAction: Apply SpO2; titrate O2 only if <94% (currently 99%, no O2 indicated)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.
expectedActions.rationale: Avoid hyperoxia in a saturating patient; supports recognition that this is rhythm-driven, not hypoxia-driven.

expectedActions.shortAction: Place on monitor; obtain 12-lead EKG; identify regular narrow-complex tachycardia (SVT)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Initiate monitoring and perform 12-lead EKG.
expectedActions.rationale: 12-lead confirms narrow-complex, regular, rate ~190 with no P waves — SVT. Rules in/out wide complex which would change drug choice.

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish IV access — prefer AC or proximal site for adenosine push
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish IV access. Adenosine 6 mg IV (proximal site) followed by 10 mL fluid bolus.
expectedActions.rationale: Adenosine has a half-life of <10 seconds; must be a proximal vein with a flush ready to reach the AV node before metabolism.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose to rule out hypoglycemia as driver
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia per the Hypoglycemia Guideline.
expectedActions.rationale: Hypoglycemia is on the differential of tachycardia per the H's and T's; quick to exclude.

expectedActions.shortAction: Targeted history for underlying cause (caffeine, stimulants, thyroid, prior episodes)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Search for underlying cause: Medications (caffeine, diet pills, thyroid, decongestants); Drugs (cocaine, amphetamines); History of dysrhythmia; Congestive heart failure (CHF).
expectedActions.rationale: Patient reports coffee and prior self-resolving episodes — supports paroxysmal SVT diagnosis.

expectedActions.shortAction: Perform vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, then modified Valsalva with supine leg elevation)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regular Narrow Complex Tachycardia – Stable (SVT): Perform vagal maneuvers.
expectedActions.rationale: First-line for stable SVT. Modified Valsalva (REVERT) has higher conversion rate than standard Valsalva.

transitions.condition: Vagal maneuvers (standard Valsalva and modified Valsalva) attempted and unsuccessful. Patient remains in SVT at HR ~190, BP holds at 110/70, mentation intact. Patient begins to verbalize increasing anxiety about his heart.

Crew misreads patient's anxiety and palpitation complaints as 'instability' and proceeds directly to synchronized cardioversion without first attempting adenosine. Patient is still alert and perfusing — cardioversion is premature and painful.

phases.name: Vagal failed, anxiety rising
phases.description: Vagal maneuvers have failed. Patient remains in regular narrow-complex tachycardia at HR ~190. Hemodynamics are still preserved — BP 110/70, alert, oriented, SpO2 99%. However, the patient is becoming visibly more anxious: increased restlessness on the stretcher, voice getting louder, complaining more intensely about chest tightness and 'feeling like I'm going to pass out.' This is the key decision point — the crew must recognize that anxiety symptoms (subjective distress, hyperventilation, restlessness) are NOT the same as hemodynamic instability (hypotension, altered mentation, ischemic chest pain with EKG changes, signs of shock). The clinically correct path is to hold the line and proceed with adenosine.
phases.triggerCondition: Standard Valsalva and modified Valsalva (REVERT) attempted; rhythm persists at ~190. Patient becomes increasingly anxious, hyperventilating slightly, verbalizing distress more loudly, but vital signs remain stable. Risk: anxiety mimics instability.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent, speaking in full sentences but voice strained
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Tachypneic from anxiety, lungs clear bilaterally, no work of breathing
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Radial pulse rapid and regular ~190, cap refill <2s, BP holding
clinicalPresentation.pupils: PERRL 4mm, slightly dilated from sympathetic surge
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities, no focal deficits, restless on stretcher
otherFindings: Repeatedly asking 'am I going to die?'
Subjective worsening of chest tightness but no new ischemic features on monitor
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'It's getting worse — please, do something! I feel like I'm going to pass out!'

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess stability: BP, mentation, perfusion — distinguish anxiety from true instability
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Presentation: Patients will manifest elevated heart rate for age and may or may not also present with associated signs or symptoms such as palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, syncope/near-syncope, hemodynamic compromise, altered mental status, or other signs of end organ malperfusion.
expectedActions.rationale: Anxiety, restlessness, subjective chest discomfort, and a louder voice are NOT instability. Instability = hypotension (BP <90), altered mentation, ischemic EKG changes, or signs of shock. BP 110/70 and A&Ox3 is still stable.

expectedActions.shortAction: Verbal reassurance, calm coaching, slow breathing — keep patient cooperative
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish patient rapport: Attempt verbal reassurance and calm patient prior to use of pharmacologic and/or physical management devices.
expectedActions.rationale: Anxiety contributes catecholamine surge that perpetuates SVT. Calming the patient is therapy, not just bedside manner.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

expectedActions.shortAction: Prepare adenosine 6 mg IV with 10 mL NS flush via stopcock; warn pt of brief 'doom' sensation
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adenosine 6 mg IV (proximal site) followed by 10 mL fluid bolus. If tachycardia continues, give adenosine 12 mg IV.
expectedActions.rationale: Adenosine half-life is <10 sec — drug must reach AV node before metabolism. Stopcock setup with a saline flush is technique-critical. Pre-briefing the patient reduces panic during the asystolic pause.

expectedActions.shortAction: Adenosine 6 mg rapid IVP, immediate 10 mL NS flush, elevate arm
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adenosine 6 mg IV (proximal site) followed by 10 mL fluid bolus.
expectedActions.rationale: First adenosine dose for stable narrow-complex regular SVT. Fixed dose — not weight-based in adults. Flush + arm elevation are essential.

expectedActions.shortAction: Capture continuous rhythm strip across the push — document conversion or persistence
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Obtain monitor strips after each intervention.
expectedActions.rationale: Documents conversion (brief asystolic pause then sinus) vs persistence. Required for hospital handoff and supports the next-dose decision.

transitions.if: Adenosine 6 mg with proper flush given
transitions.condition: Adenosine 6 mg pushed rapid IVP at proximal site with immediate 10 mL saline flush and arm elevation. Brief asystolic pause on monitor, then rhythm breaks to sinus tachycardia ~105, BP rises slightly, patient feels the 'flush' but recovers within seconds.

transitions.if: Adenosine given without rapid flush or arm elevation
transitions.condition: Adenosine 6 mg pushed but flush is slow, delayed, or omitted (no stopcock setup, no arm elevation). Drug is metabolized before reaching the AV node. No conversion. Crew may incorrectly conclude the SVT is 'refractory' when the real problem was administration technique.

Crew misinterprets patient's escalating anxiety and verbal distress as hemodynamic instability and proceeds to synchronized cardioversion without giving adenosine. Patient is still alert, BP 110/70, perfusing — does not meet protocol criteria for unstable narrow-complex tachycardia.

phases.name: Converted to sinus
phases.description: Brief asystolic pause on the monitor (3–5 seconds) followed by spontaneous return to sinus rhythm at ~105 bpm. Patient describes a brief 'doom' or 'flushing' sensation that resolves within seconds. Chest tightness eases. BP rises to 124/78. SpO2 holds at 99%. Patient is dramatically calmer once he realizes he's better. Continue reassessment en route to the ED — recurrence is possible, so monitor continuously and have a second dose of adenosine and pads ready.
phases.triggerCondition: Adenosine 6 mg pushed rapid IVP with immediate 10 mL flush and arm elevation. Brief AV nodal block produces a transient asystolic pause; sinus node resumes control. SVT terminated.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR settling, lungs clear, no distress
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Radial pulse regular ~105, cap refill <2s, skin warm and pink
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities, settled on stretcher
otherFindings: Brief 3-5 second pause on rhythm strip just prior to conversion
Mild fatigue but no recurrent symptoms
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'Whoa — that was awful for a second, but I can feel it stopped. Thank you.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Repeat 12-lead post-conversion; document baseline rhythm and intervals
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance of serial EKGs is encouraged for symptomatic patients with EKGs initially non-diagnostic for STEMI.
expectedActions.rationale: Post-conversion 12-lead can reveal underlying WPW (delta wave, short PR) which would change long-term management and warn of recurrence risk.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous cardiac monitor, ready for recurrence; second-dose adenosine (12 mg) drawn up
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If tachycardia continues, give adenosine 12 mg IV.
expectedActions.rationale: Paroxysmal SVT can recur; have the 12 mg dose ready and pads pre-placed in case of deterioration.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to appropriate ED; advance notification with rhythm strips
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Transport and destination decisions should be based on local resources and system of care.
expectedActions.rationale: Patient needs ED evaluation for electrophysiology workup, even after successful field conversion.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document rhythm pre/post, time and dose of adenosine, response to interventions
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Initial rhythm and all rhythm changes; Time, dose, and response to medications given; Obtain monitor strips after each intervention.
expectedActions.rationale: Quality field documentation directly drives ED disposition and EP referral.

phases.name: Adenosine without proper flush
phases.description: Adenosine 6 mg was pushed but the saline flush was slow, the stopcock wasn't preloaded, or the arm wasn't elevated. The drug was metabolized in the peripheral veins before reaching the AV node. No conversion. Rhythm remains regular narrow-complex at ~192. Patient experienced no 'flush' sensation — a tell that the dose didn't make it centrally. The trap is concluding 'refractory SVT' and escalating prematurely. The correct response is to recognize the technique failure, regroup with a proper setup, and give 12 mg with rapid flush and elevation.
phases.triggerCondition: Adenosine 6 mg given without immediate rapid 10 mL saline flush and/or without raising the arm. Half-life <10 seconds means the drug never reached the AV node. No asystolic pause on the strip — diagnostic clue that the drug did not reach the heart.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Persistent rapid regular pulse ~192, BP holding 108/70
otherFindings: Patient reports NO unusual sensation during the push — red flag the drug didn't reach centrally
No transient asystole on rhythm strip
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'Did anything happen? I didn't feel anything weird.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize technique failure (no flush sensation, no rhythm pause) — not true refractory SVT
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adenosine 6 mg IV (proximal site) followed by 10 mL fluid bolus.
expectedActions.rationale: The absence of any patient sensation and the absence of a brief asystolic pause on the strip both indicate the drug did not reach the AV node. The fix is technique, not dose escalation to non-adenosine agents.

expectedActions.shortAction: Set up three-way stopcock at proximal IV; preload 10 mL saline flush
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adenosine 6 mg IV (proximal site) followed by 10 mL fluid bolus.
expectedActions.rationale: Stopcock technique allows adenosine and flush to be pushed in immediate sequence with no dead time.

expectedActions.shortAction: Adenosine 12 mg rapid IVP, immediate 10 mL flush, elevate arm
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If tachycardia continues, give adenosine 12 mg IV.
expectedActions.rationale: Per protocol, second dose is 12 mg with the same technique requirements. If technique is now correct, expect conversion.

phases.name: Cardioverted while stable
phases.description: Crew interpreted patient's escalating anxiety and verbal distress as hemodynamic instability and proceeded to synchronized cardioversion despite preserved BP (110/70), preserved mentation (A&Ox3), and preserved perfusion. This is a protocol deviation — the patient did not meet criteria for unstable narrow-complex tachycardia. Cardioversion was applied without procedural sedation (or with rushed sedation). Patient experienced a painful shock and is now traumatized, agitated, and tearful. The shock may briefly convert the rhythm, but the iatrogenic harm — pain, psychological trauma, breach of trust — was avoidable. This is a teaching branch about confusing subjective distress with objective instability.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew elected synchronized cardioversion on a patient who was still alert, perfusing, and normotensive. Anxiety symptoms were misread as instability. Protocol criteria for unstable narrow-complex tachycardia (hypotension, AMS, ischemic chest pain, shock) were NOT met.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Tachypneic from pain and panic, lungs clear
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Rhythm may have converted briefly to sinus tachy ~115; BP 130/85 (pain response)
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Pulling away from crew, guarding chest where pads were
otherFindings: Patient tearful, distressed: 'You shocked me — I was awake!'
Loss of patient trust — refusing further care for several minutes
Risk of recurrence remains; this was a procedural deviation
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'Why did you do that?! I was talking to you! That hurt so much — I thought you were helping me.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize protocol deviation — pt was stable; cardioversion not indicated
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regular Narrow Complex Tachycardia – Stable (SVT): Perform vagal maneuvers; Adenosine 6 mg IV (proximal site) followed by 10 mL fluid bolus.
expectedActions.rationale: Stable SVT pathway is vagal → adenosine 6 mg → adenosine 12 mg. Cardioversion is reserved for UNSTABLE tachycardia (hypotension, AMS, ischemic CP, shock). Anxiety is not instability.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess rhythm, perfusion, mental status, and trauma response post-shock
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension.
expectedActions.rationale: Even an unnecessary shock requires full reassessment — rhythm may be transient, pad-site injury, psychological trauma must all be addressed.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reestablish rapport, calm reassurance, acknowledge distress; document event accurately
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish patient rapport: Attempt verbal reassurance and calm patient prior to use of pharmacologic and/or physical management devices.
expectedActions.rationale: Trust repair is clinically necessary — patient needs to remain cooperative for ongoing monitoring, IV access, transport. Documentation must reflect what actually happened.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency
